name: Seymour Papert
key_contributions: Co-authored "Perceptrons" (1969) with Marvin Minsky — demonstrated limitations of single-layer perceptrons
The "Perceptrons" book contributed to the first AI winter by dampening enthusiasm for neural networks
Created the Logo programming language for children's education
Pioneered constructionism — the theory that learning happens through building things
Co-founded the MIT AI Lab with Minsky
summary: Seymour Papert was a mathematician, computer scientist, and educator who co-authored 'Perceptrons' (1969) with Marvin Minsky. This book rigorously demonstrated the mathematical limitations of single-layer perceptrons, showing they could not compute simple functions like XOR. While technically correct, the book's conclusions were widely (mis)interpreted as condemning all neural network research, contributing directly to the first AI winter. Papert's legacy is thus paradoxical: he helped trigger the near-death of connectionism, yet the field's eventual resurrection proved far more powerful than what he had critiqued.
